title: Week 13 Practices (0519)
text:
Light and L are tracking a new Killer, a person who owns a Death Note and can cause people to die of cardiac arrest by writing their names in it. After this new Killer appears, Light notices something unusual. The Killer does not only write the names of criminals, but also writes the names of top managers from large companies. Light begins to suspect that the new Killer may belong to a large company and may be using the Death Note for private profit. If this hypothesis is correct, then the stock price of the Killer’s company should increase after each suspicious death.
To investigate this possibility, Light collects daily stock prices for several companies and tracks how their prices change after each death. The dataset contains:

company: The company’s name.
date: The date.
price: The stock price.

The suspicious deaths happened once per week:



Event
Date
Top manager killed




1
2026-04-06
Ryo Kamei


2
2026-04-13
Naomi Ogawa


3
2026-04-20
Keiko Arima


4
2026-04-27
Shun Takeda



If Light’s hypothesis is correct, then there should be repeated stock price surges in the Killer’s company. Follow the instructions below to help Light test this hypothesis.

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj.
Open a new script and save it in the script folder.
Download the dataset death_note.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)
Install and call the packages tidyverse, fixest.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Read death_note.csv and save it as death_note_raw. Then create a new dataframe called death_note_tidy by converting date into a Date variable.
Create a vector called death_dates that stores the four death dates. Then use geom_line() to draw the stock price trends over time and add red dashed vertical lines for the four deaths, and facet the plot by company with the setting scales = "free_y". Which of the company looks the most suspicious?
Create four post-death dummy variables: after_kamei_death, after_ogawa_death, after_arima_death, and after_takeda_death. Each variable should be equal to 1 if the date is on or after the corresponding death date, and 0 otherwise.
Create a vector called companies that stores the company names. Then create an empty dataframe called suspect_results.
Use a for loop to run one TWFE regression for each company. For each loop, create a dummy variable called target_company, which equals 1 for the company currently being checked and 0 otherwise. Estimate the following model using feols():


\begin{align*}
price_{it}
=
\alpha_i
+
\lambda_t &+\beta_1 target_i \times after\_kamei\_death_t
+
\beta_2 target_i \times after\_ogawa\_death_t  \\
&+
\beta_3 target_i \times after\_arima\_death_t
+
\beta_4 target_i \times after\_takeda\_death_t
+
u_{it}.
\end{align*}

After each regression, extract the four estimated coefficients and save the company name, event date, and coefficient estimate into suspect_results.

Add a column called mean_increase to suspect_results that calculates the average estimated stock price increase for each company. Then use arrange() to sort the companies in decreasing order of mean_increase. Create a variable called most_suspicious_company that stores the company with the highest value of mean_increase.
For most_suspicious_company, print the TWFE regression summary above twice:

once with homoscedastic errors,
once with standard errors clustered by company.


Do the regression results match your observation in Step 2? Which method gives smaller standard errors overall?

After each regression, extract the four estimated coefficients and save the company name, event date, and coefficient estimate into suspect_results.

once with homoscedastic errors,

title: Week 11 Practices (0505)
text:
Tokai Teio (東海帝王) and Mejiro McQueen (目白麥昆) are preparing for the Emperor’s Prize (天皇賞), one of the highest-level races in horse racing.
Tokai Teio has been outstanding at middle distances and remains undefeated, but she suffered a fracture a few months ago and is now preparing for her comeback. Mejiro McQueen is known for her strength at longer distances and is seeking to defend her title in the Emperor’s Prize. Their showdown in the Emperor’s Prize has become the center of attention for horse racing fans.
In this practice, we use the training data of Tokai Teio and Mejiro McQueen to predict the race outcome. The dataset contains:

date: The date of the training session.
runner: The runner’s name (Tokai Teio or Mejiro McQueen).
distance_m: The distance in meters.
final_time: The recorded finishing time, stored as a character string in m:ss.s format.

Given that the Emperor’s Prize is a 3200-meter race, who is predicted to win the Emperor’s Prize based on the training data?

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save it in the script folder.
Download the dataset pretty_derby.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)
Call the packages tidyverse and estimatr.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Read pretty_derby.csv and save it as pretty_derby_raw. Then create a new dataframe called pretty_derby_tidy by copying pretty_derby_raw.
Clean the data pretty_derby_tidy:

Convert date to Date type.
Create a numeric variable race_time_sec that measures final_time in second using sub().
Create distance_km = distance_m / 1000.
Create after_fracture, equal to 1 for Tokai Teio after 1992-01-10, and 0 otherwise.








Hint. (Try it yourself before you open it!)





Use sub(":.*", "", x) to extract min_part from final_time.




Make a plot called time_distance_plot to show the relationship between distance_m and race_time_sec, colored by runner. Add a second-order polynomial smooth line using geom_smooth() with:

method = "lm"
formula = y ~ x + I(x^2)
se = FALSE

Fit a robust linear regression model called model_pretty_derby using lm_robust() with:

response variable: race_time_sec
interaction term between runner and distance_km
interaction term between runner and the square ofdistance_km
the control variable after_fracture


Based on the context and the results, do you think it is reasonable to include the polynomial terms?

Create a dataframe called emperor_size_prediction with three rows:

Tokai Teio at 3.2 km with after_fracture = 1
Tokai Teio at 3.2 km with after_fracture = 0
Mejiro McQueen at 3.2 km with after_fracture = 0


Then use predict() with to add a column predicted_time_sec for these three cases and answer the following questoins:

Who is predicted to be faster in the Emperor Prize?
If Tokai Teio had not been injured, would your prediction changed?

text:

Hint. (Try it yourself before you open it!)

Make a plot called time_distance_plot to show the relationship between distance_m and race_time_sec, colored by runner. Add a second-order polynomial smooth line using geom_smooth() with:

Fit a robust linear regression model called model_pretty_derby using lm_robust() with:

title: Week 7 Practices (0407)
text:
Kaiji is gambling with Tonegawa in E-Card.






E-Card Game Rule





In E-Card, one side holds 4 Citizen cards and 1 Emperor card, while the other side holds 4 Citizen cards and 1 Slave card. Each player plays one card in each round.
The rule is:

Emperor beats Citizen
Citizen beats Slave
Slave beats Emperor

If the Slave catches the Emperor in a round, the player on the slave side wins. Otherwise, the player on the emperor side wins. Because the slave_side is at a disadvantage, winning from that side is much harder.



As the games go on, Kaiji appears to be dominated by Tonegawa, so he begins to suspect that Tonegawa is cheating. He records some of his feelings and observations during the game. The dataset contains the following variables (in this practice, you need to generate the dataset yourself):

game_num: The game number from 1 to 50.
side: Kaiji’s side in that round, either emperor_side or slave_side. Kaiji’s side in each round is random: it is emperor_side if X < 0.5, where X \sim \text{Unif}(0,1).
kaiji_stress: Kaiji’s stress level during the round. He feels more stressed on the emperor_side, so kaiji_stress follows N(0.5, 0.4^2) on the slave_side and N(0.7, 0.4^2) on the emperor_side.
tonegawa_pressure: The pressure from Tonegawa during the round. It follows N(0.5, 0.3^2).
decision_time: How long Kaiji hesitates before making his decision (by minutes). It follows \text{Exp}(2).
if_win: Whether Kaiji wins the round (1 = win, 0 = lose). This variable is generated by a helper function saved in calc_win.R, which depends on side, kaiji_stress, tonegawa_pressure, and decision_time. (right click here)


Kaiji suspects that Tonegawa is using some illegal method to infer his strategy, for example, by detecting his blood pressure. If Kaiji’s hypothesis is correct, then his win probability should depend on the stress level he feels, but not on how confident Tonegawa is or how long he takes to make a decision.
Follow the instructions below to help Kaiji test his hypothesis:

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save it in the script folder.
Make sure the helper file calc_win.R is saved in the script folder.
Install and call the packages tidyverse, estimatr, and car.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Set the random seed to 123, and let the number of rounds be 50.
Create a dataframe called e_card_data that contains game_num, side, kaiji_stress, tonegawa_pressure, and decision_time. Standardize the last three variables using scale().
Source the helper file calc_win.R and combine the output into e_card_data (do not copy and paste the function inside!).
Fit a robust linear regression model called reg_win with if_win as the response variable and kaiji_stress, tonegawa_pressure, decision_time, and side as the predictors. Print the regression summary.
Test the null hypothesis H_0:\beta_1 = \beta_2 where:

\beta_1 is the coefficient on kaiji_stress
\beta_2 is the coefficient on tonegawa_pressure Can you reject the null hypothesis that the coefficients on kaiji_stress and tonegawa_pressure are equal?

Change the number of random seed (to any number you like), does the result stays similar?

title: Week 5 Practices (0324)
text:
In this practice, we will use the (simulated) data from the 2025 Statistics with Recitation course to examine whether students’ quiz scores can be good predictors of their midterm scores.






Note



This dataset is not the raw data from the course. I simulated the data based on the raw data and made sure that the new dataset generates results very similar to those of the original dataset.


The dataset contains:

ID: The ID number of each student.
section: The section each student belongs to.
midterm, quiz_1, quiz_2, quiz_3: The corresponding midterm and quiz scores, where midterm scores range from 0 to 100 and quiz scores range from 0 to 30.

Please follow the instructions below to conduct the analysis. (Take a guess before you do it!)

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save in the script folder.
Download the dataset grades.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)

Call the package tidyverse.
Create an output folder inside the project folder if you do not have one.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Read grades.csv and save it as grades_raw.
Replace all blank cells in grades_raw with NA.







Hint



Use grades_raw[grades_raw == ""] <- NA.



Create a new dataframe called grades_tidy from grades_raw with the following steps:

Keep only the rows where section is "國立臺灣大學修課學生 (ECON2022-03)".
Remove the columns ID and section.
Remove rows with missing values using na.omit().
Convert midterm, quiz_1, quiz_2, and quiz_3 to numeric type.
Create a new column avg_quiz_score equal to the average of quiz_1, quiz_2, and quiz_3.

Rename the columns of grades_tidy as follows: midterm, quiz_ch_1to2, quiz_ch_3to4, quiz_r, avg_quiz_score.
First, let’s see if there’s relationship between students’ midterm scores and their average quiz scores. Make a scatter plot with avg_quiz_score on the x-axis, midterm on the y-axis, and with the settings:

x-axis: "Average Quiz Score", ranges from 0 to 30
y-axis: "Midterm Score", ranges from 0 to 100
color: "steelblue"
size: 2.5
Labels: x "Average Quiz Score", y "Midterm Score"

Print the correlation between midterm and avg_quiz_score.
Fit a linear regression model called reg_midterm_on_avg with midterm as the response variable and avg_quiz_score as the predictor. Save its summary as reg_midterm_on_avg_summary, and print the summary. What is the result? Does it match what you see in the scatter plot?
Next, let us investigate further to see which quiz serves as the best predictor of the midterm score. Fit a linear regression model called reg_midterm_on_all_quiz with midterm as the response variable and quiz_ch_1to2, quiz_ch_3to4, and quiz_r as the predictors. Save its summary as reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary, and print the summary. How would you explain the regression result?
Creating a plot reg_midterm_on_avg_plot by adding a regression line (in step 6) to the plot you just created in step 5 with the following settings and then print it.

slope: the coefficient of avg_quiz_score from reg_midterm_on_avg_summary
intercept: the intercept from reg_midterm_on_avg_summary
color: "lightcoral"
linetype: "dashed"

Save reg_midterm_on_avg_plot into an output folder as a png file named reg_midterm_on_avg_plot.png using ggsave() with the settings: width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 300.

title: Week 3 Practices (0310)
text:
Open the project you create in the first week.
Create a new script for today’s exercise and save in the script folder.

title: Week 3 Practices (0310)
text:
Open the project you create in the first week.
Create a new script for today’s exercise and save in the script folder.

title: Week 3 Practices (0310)
text:
2 GNOSIA (古諾希亞)
Yuri is trapped in a time loop! In each loop, there are 15 possible people who may appear. After repeating the loop many times, he begins to suspect that Yuriko appears more often than the other people. To investigate this, Yuri decides to record which people appear in each of the next 50 loops. The dataset contains:

loop: The loop number.
Yuri, Setsu, Gina, SQ, Raqio, Stella, Shigemichi, Chipie, Comet, Jonas, Kukrushka, Otome, Sha-Ming, Remnan, Yuriko: Each of these columns records whether the person appeared in that loop, using 1 for “appeared” and 0 for “did not appear”.

Yuri wants to know: after how many loops can he reject the null hypothesis with p < 0.0001 that Yuriko has the same appearance rate as the other people?
Follow the instructions below to help Yuri find the answer:

Download the dataset gnosia.csv. (right click here) Then call the package tidyverse.
Read gnosia.csv and save it as gnosia_raw.
Because Setsu is also a time looper, she will appear in each loop as well. To adjust this, create a new dataframe called gnosia_tidy by removing the columns loop, Yuri, and Setsu in gnosia_raw.







Note



In steps 3-6, we will create a function t_test_pval() to help us calculate the p-value from the t-test for a given loop data.



Write a function called t_test_pval() that takes a dataframe data as input.
Inside t_test_pval(), create a new column appear_sum to data that calculates the row sum of all columns in the input dataframe.
Still inside t_test_pval(), create a new column not_Yuriko_appear_rate, defined as the average appearance rate of the 12 people other than Yuriko.







Hint



Subtract Yuriko from appear_sum, then divide by 12.



Inside t_test_pval(), use an paired sample t.test() to compare Yuriko and not_Yuriko_appear_rate, then extract and return the p-value p.
Before the loop starts, create two variables:

minimal_loop, with initial value 0.
finish_index, with initial value 0.

Use a for loop from 2 to 50. In each iteration:

Keep only the first i rows of gnosia_tidy and save it as current_loop_data.
Pass current_loop_data into t_test_pval() and save the returned value as pval.

Inside the for loop, check whether: pval < 0.0001, and finish_index == 0. If both conditions are true, save i as minimal_loop, and change finish_index to 1.
Print the value pval in each loop.
After the for loop ends, print minimal_loop. What is the smallest number of loops needed for Yuri to reject the null hypothesis?

title: Week 2 Practices (0303)
text:
2 Chou Kaguya-hime! (超時空輝耀姬！)
Kaguya is a new liver in Tsukuyomi. Her roommate, Iroha, also serves as her manager. To improve Kaguya’s streaming performance, Iroha records data from each stream, including:

date: The date of the stream.
content: The type of content Kaguya did during the stream.
paired_with_iroha: Sometimes Iroha joins Kaguya’s stream. If Iroha appeared in the stream on that date, record Yes; otherwise, record No.
hours: The duration of the stream (in hours).
donation: The total amount of donations Kaguya received.

Tsukuyomi takes 30% of the donations as a commission. Iroha wants to know which type of content has the highest earnings per hour when Kaguya streams alone.
Follow the instructions below to help Iroha find the answer:

Use the pipe operator %>% appropriately in the steps below and download the dataset kaguya.csv. (right click here) Then install and call the package tidyverse.
Read kaguya.csv and save it as kaguya_raw.
Without modifying kaguya_raw, create a new dataframe called kaguya_solo by filtering the data to keep only the streams where Kaguya streamed alone (i.e., not paired with Iroha).
Add a new column earning to kaguya_solo, defined as 70% of donation.
Add a new column earning_per_hour to kaguya_solo, calculated as earning / hours.
Keep only the columns date, content, earning_per_hour in kaguya_solo.
Arrange the rows of kaguya_solo in descending order of earning_per_hour.
Print kaguya_solo. Which date’s stream has the highest earnings per hour?
Create a new dataframe kaguya_solo_sum that summarizes the average earning_per_hour for each content category, and arranges it in the descending order of average earning_per_hour. Which type of content has the highest average earnings per hour?

title: Week 4 Practices (0317)
text:
Inori is practicing for the Chubu Regional Championships, a figure skating competition in Japan. Her coach, Tsukasa, records the result of each jump attempt every day during practice. The dataset contains:

date: The date of the practice session.
jump: The type of figure skating jump.
result: The landing outcome of the jump attempt.

On March 23, Inori caught a mild cold, and Tsukasa noticed that her landing performance seemed to be affected. Follow the instructions below to help Tsukasa test this hypothesis.

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save in the script folder.
Download the dataset medalist.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)

Call the package tidyverse.
Create an output folder inside the project folder.

Read medalist.csv and save it as medalist_raw.
Create a variable called cold_date equal to 2026-03-23 as a Date.
Create a new dataframe called medalist_tidy from medalist_raw with the following columns:

Convert date column to Date type using as.Date().
Create is_landing column: 1 if the jump result is Landing, otherwise 0.
Convert result column type into factor with level order: Fall, Touch down, Landing.
Create is_after_cold column: "before_cold" if the date is before Inori’s cold, otherwise "after_cold".
Convert is_after_cold column type into factor with level order: before_cold, after_cold.








Note



In steps 4-5, we create a histogram to show the distribution of how many jump Inori did each day.



Create a dataframe called medalist_daily that summarizes how many jumps Inori did each day.







Hint



Calculate the number of rows in each group date using summarise(jump_times = n())



Make a histogram called medalist_hist_plot that shows the distribution of jump_times and print it with the settings: binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "black".







Note



In steps 6-7, we create a bar plot to compare the performance of Inori’s jump by calculate the count of the jumping results before and after the cold.



Make a bar plot called medalist_bar_plot and print it with the settings:

x-axis: result
fill: is_after_cold
Labels: title Jump results, x Result, y Count.

Make a faceted bar plot by jump called medalist_facet_bar_plot with setting in Step 6 with title, Jump results (by jump type).







Note



In steps 8-10, we create a line graph to compare the performance of Inori’s jump by calculate the landing success rate of the jumps before and after the cold.



Make a line plot called medalist_line_plot showing daily average landing success rate and print it with the settings:

Lines in plot: linewidth = 0.7, color = "steelblue".
Add a vertical line at cold_date in color lightcoral.
Add a horizontal line at y = 0.8 in color lightcoral.
Labels: title "Daily average landing success rate", x "Date", y "Success rate".

Create a dataframe medalist_success_rate_by_date_jump that summarizes the average landing success rate per jump each at each date.
Make a faceted line plot by jump called medalist_facet_line_plot using medalist_success_rate_by_date_jump and print it with the settings:


Lines in plot: linewidth = 0.7, color = "steelblue".
Add a vertical line at cold_date in color lightcoral.
Add a horizontal line at y = 0.8 in color lightcoral.
Labels: title "Daily landing success rate (by jump)", x "Date", y "Success rate".


Save all five plots into an output folder as png files using ggsave() with the settings: width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 300. Additionally, save an extra graph medalist_facet_line_plot_dpi150 for medalist_facet_line_plot with setting width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 150. Do you see the differences between these two graphs?
Based on your plots, answer the questions:


Did the landing success rate appear to change after cold_date?
Are some jump types less affected than others?

Full Performance Youtube Link

Full Performance Youtube Link

title: Week 6 Practices (0331)
text:
Lawrence and Holo goes from town to town to make profits as a travelling merchant. To decide where bread is relatively expensive or cheap, Lawrence records the quoted market prices of several goods in 24 towns and cities along the route.
The dataset contains:

place: The name of the town or city.
region: The broad trading region of the market (North, Central, or South).
market_type: The type of market (village, market_town, church_city, port_city, or spa_town).
bread_price: The quoted bread price in each market.
wheat_price: The quoted wheat price in each market.
rye_price: The quoted rye price in each market.
cheese_price: The quoted cheese price in each market.
butter_price: The quoted butter price in each market.

Prices are recorded as character strings, and some markets use the currency unit trenni while others use lumione. The exchange rate is 1 lumione = 4 trenni.
In this world, rye is often treated as a substitute for wheat, while cheese and butter are commonly sold and consumed together with bread. Hence, Lawrence wants to know how the price of bread is related to the prices of wheat, rye, cheese, and butter. Follow the instructions below to help Lawrence study these price relationships.

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save it in the script folder.
Download the dataset spice_and_wolf.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)
Install and call the packages tidyverse, estimatr, and texreg.
Create an output folder inside the project folder if you do not already have one.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Read spice_and_wolf.csv and save it as spice_and_wolf_raw. Then create a new dataframe called spice_and_wolf_tidy by copying spice_and_wolf_raw.
Convert all price columns in spice_and_wolf_tidy from mixed currency strings into numeric prices measured in trenni. (1 lumione = 4 trenni)







Hint. (Try it yourself before you open it!)





Check whether the value in price columns contains "trenni" or "lumione" for each row inside a for loop.

If it contains "trenni", remove the last 6 characters.
If it contains "lumione", remove the last 7 characters and multiply the result by 4.
Make sure that all price columns are of numeric type.





Create a two-way table of region and market_type, and add margins to the table.
Fit a linear regression model called reg_bread_on_wheat with bread_price as the response variable and wheat_price as the predictor. Print the regression summary.
Fit a linear regression model called reg_bread_on_all with bread_price as the response variable and wheat_price, rye_price, cheese_price, and butter_price as the predictors. Print the regression summary. Does the coefficient on wheat_price change?
Fit a robust linear regression model called reg_bread_on_all_robust using the same formula as in Step 5. Print the regression summary. How does the result differ from reg_bread_on_all?
Compare reg_bread_on_wheat, reg_bread_on_all, and reg_bread_on_all_robust using screenreg().

title: Week 8 Practices (0414)
text:

title: Week 12 Practices (0512)
text:
Hiraku manages many fields in the village. Some fields are cultivated by Hiraku using the Almighty Farming Tool (萬能農具), while other fields are cultivated by ordinary farming methods. The information of each field are documented by Fraurem inside a csv. The dataset contains:

plot_id: The ID of the field.
crop: The crop planted in the field.
field_size_m2: The size of the field, measured in square meters.
almighty_farming_tool: Whether the field was cultivated using the Almighty Farming Tool.
harvest_result_2023: The amount of crops harvested in 2023, measured in sacks.
harvest_result_2024: The amount of crops harvested in 2024, measured in sacks.
harvest_result_2025: The amount of crops harvested in 2025, measured in sacks.

Hiraku plans to grow a new tomato field, and he wants to know how much the predicted probability of becoming a high-yield field increases if the field is cultivated with the Almighty Farming Tool. Follow the instructions below to help Hiraku study these questions.

Before you begin, complete the setup:

Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on .Rproj (if you do not have one, create it).
Open a new script and save it in the script folder.
Download the dataset farming.csv and save it in the data folder. (right click here)
Install and call the packages tidyverse, estimatr, and pscl.
(Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using rm(list = ls()).

Read farming.csv and save it as farming_raw. Then create a new dataframe called farming_tidy by copying farming_raw.
Use pivot_longer() to reshape the harvest columns into long form. The new dataset should have:

one row for each field-year,
a column called harvest_year,
a column called harvest_result.

Create a new variable called high_yield_field. Let high_yield_field be equal to 1 if harvest_result is larger than 50, and 0 otherwise.
Fit a logarithmic regression model called reg_log_harvest using lm_robust(). The response variable is log(harvest_result), and the predictors are field_size_m2, almighty_farming_tool, and crop. Print the regression summary.
Fit a logistic regression model called logit_high_yield using glm(). The response variable is high_yield_field, and the predictors are field_size_m2, almighty_farming_tool, and crop. Print the regression summary, and use pR2() to calculate the pseudo-R^2 of the logistic regression model.
Create a new dataframe called new_tomato_fields with two tomato fields of 3000 square meters: one cultivated with the Almighty Farming Tool and one cultivated without it. Use logit_high_yield and predict() to calculate the probability of each field to be a high_yield_field.
